message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "National Life Group (NLG) / Life Insurance Company of the Southwest (LSW) TERM LIFE INSURANCE products — comprehensive product reference. Cover: (1) the full current term lineup and product names (e.g. the "G" guaranteed level-premium series Term 10-G / 15-G / 20-G / 30-G, form series ICC18-20522, and any other current term products like LSW Level Term / annually-renewable term); (2) issue ages and available level term lengths; (3) the level-premium period then annually-renewable-to-age-95 structure, whether the policy has cash value/dividends (it does not); (4) the conversion privilege — window (first 20 years or to age 70 if earlier), no evidence of insurability, equivalent rate class, what permanent products it converts to; (5) the Accelerated Benefit Rider (ABR) living-benefit suite available on term (Terminal, Chronic, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, Alzheimer's) — cost (no additional premium), triggers, discounted/condition-specific payout, and per-insured lifetime benefit limits and how they vary by state; (6) premium structure and rate classes (Elite/Preferred/Select/Standard/Express, tobacco/non-tobacco); (7) state availability variations; (8) foreign-national / non-US-resident rules for term (minimum face, US nexus/solicitation/delivery requirements, whether Brazil/"B" country is eligible, whether term is even offered to foreign nationals). Prioritize NLG/LSW primary sources: nationallife.com, the Life Product Quick Reference Chart, agent guides, product guides, rider brochures, and the Foreign National Guidelines. Distinguish guaranteed/structural facts from rate-sheet-driven current rates (which change often). Produce a cited report with source URLs."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://www.cpasy.com/files/82918/im_10743.pdf
**Title:** Accelerated Benefit Riders — Your Guide to Living Benefits (20-page NLG guide, IM10743)
**Found via:** living benefits — ABR rider suite search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use when: morning brief, daily brief, set up my morning brief, what's my day look like, run my brief.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the PDF content to extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract details about National Life Group / LSW Accelerated Benefit Riders (living benefits). Specifically: which riders are included (Terminal Illness, Chronic Illness, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, Alzheimer's/ADL), whether there is additional premium/cost, the triggers/qualifying events, how the accelerated benefit payout is discounted/determined, per-insured lifetime benefit maximums and whether they vary by state, and any mention of availability on term life products. Quote exact language where possible. Also note any publish date or form number.

content.content:
I appreciate your request, but I must be transparent: the content provided appears to be a PDF file encoded in binary/compressed format that I cannot reliably parse or extract readable text from. The page content shows PDF stream objects, metadata, and encoded image data rather than plain, accessible text about National Life Group's accelerated benefit riders.

To provide you with accurate information about:
- Which riders are included (Terminal Illness, Chronic Illness, Critical Illness, etc.)
- Premium costs and qualifying triggers
- Benefit payout calculations and maximums
- State variations
- Term life product availability

I would need either:
1. A readable text version of the document
2. A plaintext summary of the relevant policy sections
3. Direct access to National Life Group's published materials on accelerated benefits

**I cannot extract the specific details you've requested from this encoded PDF format.** Attempting to do so would risk providing inaccurate information. I recommend accessing the original document through National Life Group directly or requesting a plaintext version of the relevant policy sections.

content.text: pdftotext worked. Let me read the extracted text.

content.content:
1	Living Benefits... When You Need Them Most
2	
3	  Accelerated Benefit Riders
4	
5	   Your Guide to Living Benefits
6	
7	                                                       Page 1 of 20
8	 What is a Living Benefit?
9	
10	American National Insurance Company's Living Benefits are Accelerated Benefit Riders (ABRs) which provide the
11	option of receiving a partial or full accelerated life insurance benefit if the insured experiences a qualifying medical
12	condition.
13	� Partial Acceleration is paid in lieu of a portion of the policy's death benefit.
14	� Full Acceleration is paid in lieu of the policy's death benefit. In the case of a full acceleration, the policy will
15	
16	    be terminated after acceleration is paid.
17	The Accelerated Benefit Riders can provide funds when they are needed to help you at a critical time and help protect
18	the money you have saved for retirement and other life needs.
19	
20	                                                                   Page 2 of 20
21	 The Accelerated Benefit Riders provide living benefit
22	options for no additional premium.1
23	
24	For many years insurance was purchased to provide death benefit protection for surviving family members. However, insurance did not help
25	when an insured was struck down by a debilitating Critical, Chronic, or Terminal illness. American National Insurance Company now offers
26	you the potential to access part of your life insurance death benefit in the event you suffer a qualifying illness and may not be able to work and
27	have medical bills piling up.
28	American National's Accelerated Benefits Riders for Critical, Chronic, and Terminal illness may provide you a full acceleration of your life
29	insurance policy or a partial benefit that will allow you to keep the remainder of your policy. Multiple partial benefits are available if a partial
30	benefit is taken. For example if 25% of the death benefit is accelerated, 75% of the death benefit would remain and could be accessed later, if
31	needed.
32	The partial or full accelerated death benefit may be paid in a lump sum or applied to an annuity that will provide income for a specified
33	period.
34	
35	     Terminal Illness
36	
37	        Imagine for a moment that you go to the Doctor for a routine test and the Doctor tells you that you have at most six months
38	        to live. Let that sink in. It would feel like you had been kicked in the gut by a mule. It would be like sitting on a stool
39	        and having someone pull it out from under you. And that same feeling will be experienced by every family member and
40	        every friend you have. How do you deal with it? And how do you deal with the fact you will be unable to return to
41	        work to provide for your family; be unable to pay all of the mounting medical bills that will come in addition to life's daily
42	        expenses? Instead of being the care-giver for your family, your family becomes your care-giver. How do you pay for all of
43	        that?
44	
45	     Chronic Illness
46	
47	        Chronic Illness is a health condition or disease that is persistent or otherwise long lasting. Chronic Illness causes about
48	        70% of deaths in the U.S.2 Nearly 92% of seniors have at least one chronic disease and 77% have two or more chronic
49	        illnesses.3 With numbers like this, the majority of Americans will experience a debilitating Chronic Illness sometime in their
50	        life. When that happens your financial world is turned upside-down. How do you pay for that?
51	
52	     Critical Illness
53	
54	        A 25 year old male non-smoker has a 24% chance of having a critical illness (cancer, heart attack, or stroke) prior to
55	        turning age 65. That same age male who smokes has a 49% chance of having a critical illness prior to age 65.4 It is not
56	        only older Americans who are susceptible to Critical Illness as younger people also suffer from cancer, heart attacks, and
57	        strokes. Whether you are young or old, the same issues can arise. You could become unable to work full-time, require
58	        family members to miss work to provide care, and face astronomical medical bills. Where does the help come from?
59	
60	  1The benefit is subject to an administrative fee of up to $500 and an actuarial discount that will be assessed when the benefit is elected.
61	    The maximum administrative fee may vary by state.
62	  2Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Chronic Disease Overview. http://www.cdc.gov/nccdphp/overview.htm. May 2014.
63	  3National Council on Aging. Chronic Disease Self Management Fact Sheet. December 2014.
64	  4National Critical Illness Risk Assessment Study, published by the American Association for Critical Illness Insurance. January 2010.
65	
66	                                                                     Page 3 of 20
67	 Certainly, carrying disability insurance and/or long term care insurance is ideal for such situations and should be considered by everyone but
68	not everyone can afford to purchase such coverage or keep the coverage in place for the rest of their lives.
69	
70	For those individuals who need life insurance coverage and are seeking protection in the event of Chronic, Critical, and Terminal Illnesses,
71	American National's Accelerated Benefit Riders may be an option to potentially provide an additional financial resource if someone becomes
72	afflicted by a qualifying illness that results in a greatly reduced life expectancy.
73	
74	The benefit these riders provide is not tied to medical bills incurred but are instead tied to the face amount of the policy, the cash value at the
75	time a claim is made, and the reduction in future life expectancy as a result of the illness. There is no restriction on how you may use the funds
76	you receive.
77	
78	When computing the amount of available benefit, American National discounts the future premiums due under the policy. If someone has a Life
79	expectancy of 24 months, the death benefit would only be reduced by a small amount to account for the loss of two premium paying years.
80	However, if someone has a debilitating condition, but a ten year life expectancy, the death benefit would be reduced by a larger amount to
81	account for the loss of 10 premium paying years.
82	
83	For example, if someone who is 50 years old has a heart attack and is back at work in three months there would not be a substantial available
84	benefit since the life expectancy was not dramatically impacted. However, if that 50 year old has a very severe heart attack that results in a
85	33% chance of living for five years, their life expectancy would be dramatically diminished resulting in a larger available benefit.
86	
87	Accelerated Benefit Riders can be a safety net if a qualifying illness arises and results in a significantly reduced life expectancy. ABRs are not a
88	substitute for long term care insurance but can provide a potential source of funds under the proper circumstances.
89	
90	                                                                   Page 4 of 20
91	 Qualifying Conditions:
92	
93	Terminal Illness         � If an eligible insured has an illness or condition that is expected to result in death
94	                              within 241 months or less.
95	Rider
96	                         � Available on all rate classes
97	(Form Series: ABR14-TM)
98	
99	Chronic Illness Rider    � If an eligible insured is unable to perform 2 of 6 activities of daily living for a
100	                              period of at least 90 days or requires constant supervision to protect from threats
101	(Form Series: ABR14-CH)       to health or safety due to severe cognitive impairment.
102	
103	                         � The ADLs include bathing, continence, dressing, eating, toileting, and
104	                              transferring.
105	
106	Critical Illness Rider   � If an eligible insured experiences a critical illness2.
107	
108	(Form Series: ABR14-CT)  � See rider for complete list of critical illnesses and definitions. Covered critical
109	                              illnesses may include:
110	
111	                             1. Heart Attack
112	                             2. Stroke
113	                             3. Invasive Cancer
114	                             4. End Stage Renal Failure
115	                             5. Major Organ Transplant
116	                             6. ALS
117	                             7. Blindness
118	                             8. Paralysis
119	                             9. Arterial Aneurysms
120	                           10. Central Nervous System Tumors
121	                           11. Major Multi-System Trauma (Major injury to three or more organs or systems) -
122	
123	                                  Not Available in California
124	                           12. AIDS - Not Available in California
125	                           13. Severe Disease of Any Organ (resulting in significantly altered life
126	
127	                                  expectancy)
128	                           14. Severe Central Nervous System Disease (Parkinson's, Huntington's, Multiple
129	
130	                                  Sclerosis, Encephalitis with long-term effects) - Not Available in California
131	                           15. Major Burns (>40% BSA, 3rd Degree)
132	                           16. Loss of Limbs
133	
134	   Minimum Death                                         Maximum Total Death Benefit
135	Benefit Requirements:                                      Available for Acceleration
136	
137	� Minimum $25,000 for Terminal Illness Rider             � $2,000,000 for issue ages 0-65
138	� Minimum $50,000 for Critical or Chronic Illness Rider  � $1,000,000 for issue ages 66+
139	
140	                                                              (including all eligible riders attached to the base policy)
141	
142	1Some states may limit the definition of terminal illness to conditions that are expected to result in death within 12 months.
143	2Claims will not be processed until at least thirty days after the qualifying illness has occurred
144	
145	                                                                    Page 5 of 20
146	 Rider Availability/Limitations:
147	
148	� The chronic and critical illness versions may not be added to policies rated higher than table 4 or with flat extra rating greater than $5.00 per
149	     thousand. (Non-applicable in California. Please see Additional California Resident Availability/Limitations below.)
150	
151	� Accelerated benefits will not be paid for conditions resulting from self-inflicted injury or attempted suicide.
152	� No accelerated benefit will be paid for any qualifying event that occurs on or before the date of issue of the base policy.
153	Policies with death benefits exceeding the maximum amount available for acceleration will still contain the Accelerated Benefit Riders; however, the owner
154	will only be able to accelerate up to the maximum amount available for acceleration. For example, on a $4,000,000 policy (where eligible), the owner
155	will only be able to accelerate $2,000,000 for an insured under 65.
156	
157	Additional California Resident Availability/Limitations:
158	
159	� A supplemental application is required to determine eligibility for critical and chronic illness riders.
160	� The chronic and critical illness versions are not available for applicants age 65 and older.
161	� Chronic illness is not available in conjunction with term coverage whether it is as the base policy or a rider.
162	
163	Death Benefit Eligible for Acceleration:
164	
165	� Primary Insured Riders are included in the Total Death Benefit to be accelerated on the Base Insured.
166	� Paid Up Additions Rider (MLPUA)
167	� ANICO Signature Term Rider (ARTR12; ULLTR13) - The ANICO Signature Term Rider also covers other insured parties
168	� The Other Insured Party death benefits are available for acceleration only if the Other Insured experiences a qualifying condition.
169	Please note that the death benefit provided by a Children's Term Rider is not eligible for acceleration.
170	
171	Accelerated Benefit Amount:
172	
173	The accelerated benefit amount is computed using the following formula where the actuarial discount reflects the cost of paying a death benefit prior to
174	the actual death of the Insured. The accelerated benefit amount will never be less than the cash value associated with the eligible death benefit.
175	
176	 Eligible Death Benefit
177	- Actuarial Discount
178	- Administrative Fee (not to exceed $5001)
179	- Pro-rata percentage of Outstanding Policy Debt2
180	= Accelerated Benefit Amount
181	Please note the Owner must submit supporting documentation and medical records including certification from the appropriate health care practitioner
182	that insured has one of the qualifying conditions. If a partial benefit is requested such as 25% and there is outstanding policy debt, 25% of the debt
183	would be deducted from the Accelerated Benefit Amount.
184	
185	1The maximum administrative fee may vary by state.
186	2The amount of outstanding policy loans plus accumulated interest will be deducted only if the qualifying insured is the insured under the base policy.
187	
188	                                                                        Page 6 of 20
189	 Living Benefits: Changing Real Lives
190	
191	The following are actual cases of individuals filing a claim for accelerated benefits under
192	 the Chronic, Critical, and Terminal Illness Riders on their life insurance policies. The
193	product, amounts in force, amount requested and final disposition are all factual but the
194	
195	        names and the specific situation has been changed to protect the recipients.
196	
197	Bonnie Thomas: $234,364.24                                         Six months later Bonnie began experiencing frequent
198	                                                                   indigestion, bloating, and severe heartburn but thought it
199	Bonnie Thomas was a mother of three whose husband Bill             was due to her love for spicy foods. Several months later
200	was a salesman for a small manufacturing company. Bonnie           Bonnie's indigestion became worse and she started suffering
201	worked for an automobile dealership in the title transfer          from nausea and vomiting. Bonnie visited the doctor who
202	department and had been there for 12 years. In 2011, Bill          ordered multiple tests including a stomach biopsy. These tests
203	and Bonnie had a good year financially and decided to meet         showed that Bonnie had stomach cancer with mastitis of the
204	with a financial advisor who advised them to purchase life         liver in which Bonnie's liver had been almost totally displaced
205	insurance to provide for the family in the event of untimely       by the tumor. The news was devastating. They cried the entire
206	death. Bill and Bonnie decided that they would each apply for      weekend and told the children Mom was very ill. Bonnie
207	a $250,000 20-Year Term insurance policy from American             started treatment and was so sick that she was unable to work
208	National Insurance Company that they could convert to a            and Bill was forced to take time from his job to take care of
209	permanent policy in the future. One of the features they liked     her. Their lives would never be the same.
210	were the Accelerated Benefit Riders that may pay a benefit if
211	they had a qualifying Chronic, Critical, or Terminal illness that  Bonnie and Bill went through their savings in months, medical
212	impacted future life expectancy.                                   bills continued to pile up, and Bonnie became stressed over
213	                                                                   the financial hemorrhaging her illness was causing. Bill talked
214	                                                                   with their financial advisor who suggested they submit a claim
215	                                                                   for the Accelerated Benefit from Bonnie's term life policy.
216	                                                                   They applied and ten days later they were e-mailed their
217	                                                                   offer for $234,364.24. They were elated with the offer and
218	                                                                   immediately accepted. In calculating the offer, the American
219	                                                                   National doctor determined that Bonnie had a 4.2% chance
220	                                                                   of living for five years. Bonnie's claim was filed for critical
221	                                                                   Illness but the offer was based upon a terminal illness benefit.

360	Page 11 of 20
361	          Frequently Asked Questions
362	
363	How much do these riders cost?
364	
365	The Accelerated Benefit Riders are offered for no additional premium; however, the accelerated benefit is reduced by an amount
366	calculated based on American National's evaluation of the insured's future expected mortality at the time the benefit is exercised as
367	well as an administrative fee of up to $500 assessed when the benefits are elected.
368	
369	The illustration shows slight, moderate, and severe impairment categories. How do you determine if
370	an illness is slight, moderate, or severe?
371	
372	Illnesses do not fall into these specific categories, these categories are just meant to provide an example of a range of payments.
373	One critical illness, such as cancer, could easily result in any of the impairment categories, depending on the severity of the
374	illness and its impact on life expectancy.
375	� A slight impairment is one that does not have a severe impact on life expectancy.
376	� A severe impairment corresponds with an illness that is almost terminal, but not quite (roughly a 3 year life expectancy).
377	� A moderate impairment would be a condition that has some impact on life expectancy but not to the same extent as a severe
378	
379	     impairment.
380	For example, early treated cancer could produce
381	a slight impairment that would produce a minimum
382	payment while advanced stage cancer could
383	produce a severe impairment with a payment close
384	to terminal, all within the cancer illness.
385	
386	Why is there not an illustration of
387	accelerated benefit rider payments on
388	term life insurance policies?
389	
390	ANICO Signature Term does not use an illustration
391	wherein current and guaranteed values are
392	shown. The illustration only shows guaranteed
393	values. Because the accelerated benefit payment
394	calculation relies on non-guaranteed values (such
395	as the interest rate used) the accelerated benefits
396	can only be shown on a policy form that shows
397	non-guaranteed values. Note: In California, chronic
398	illness is not available in conjunction with term
399	coverage whether it is as the base policy or a rider.
400	
401	                                                                Page 12 of 20
402	      What amount can I expect to be offered if the qualifying illness is a heart attack?
403	
404	       The benefit amount you can expect will vary with the severity of the heart attack and its impact on future life expectancy. For example,
405	       if the doctor tells you that with proper care you should live a reasonably long life, this would likely be in the "slight" category that would
406	       result in only the cash value in the policy being available. However, if the doctor indicated a very limited life expectancy due to the
407	       severity of the heart attack, then the death benefit would be discounted less and a larger benefit would be available.
408	
409	     How is the benefit calculated?
410	
411	       The death benefit accelerated is reduced by:
412	       � an actuarial discount,
413	       � an administrative fee (not to exceed $5001) and,
414	       � a pro-rata percentage of outstanding policy debt2
415	       The size of the actuarial discount is primarily dictated by the expected future mortality of the insured. A shorter life expectancy will result
416	       in a smaller discount and a larger end payment. The actuarial discount represents the cost to the company of paying the benefit before
417	       the actual death of the insured. This discount includes the value of all premiums the company expects to receive prior to the death of the
418	       insured as well as lost investment income. Additionally, the actuarial discount can vary based on how well a policy is funded. A policy
419	       that is close to lapsing will have a larger discount than one that is well funded (because less future premiums are expected or required).
420	
421	     What factors affect the amount of the actuarial discount?
422	
423	       The following factors will affect the amount of the actuarial discount relative to the eligible death benefit.
424	       � Accelerated Benefit Interest Rate: The greater the interest rate, the larger the actuarial discount will be. The accelerated benefit
425	
426	            interest rate is determined by American National subject to a maximum value defined in the riders.
427	       � Severity of the Qualifying Medical Condition: The severity of the qualifying medical condition will determine the mortality
428	
429	            table used by us to determine the actuarial discount. The terminal illness version will use a standard mortality table for terminally
430	            ill insureds, determined by us, in computing the actuarial discount. The chronic and critical illness versions will use a custom
431	            mortality table, determined by us, based on our evaluation of the qualifying insured's specific health condition. In general, the
432	            terminal illness version is expected to result in the smallest actuarial discount. Qualifying medical conditions with higher future
433	            expected mortality rates will result in smaller actuarial discounts than conditions with lower future expected mortality rates. It is
434	            possible that minor qualifying conditions will result in an accelerated benefit amount that is equal to or only slightly greater than
435	            the cash value associated with the eligible death benefit.
436	       � Future Premiums: The greater the premium required to keep the eligible death benefit in force, the greater the actuarial
437	            discount will be.
438	       � Future Anticipated Dividends: The actuarial discount will be decreased by the present value of future anticipated dividends, if
439	            any, for your policy.
440	
441	1The maximum administrative fee may vary by state.
442	2The amount of outstanding policy loans plus accumulated interest will be deducted only if the qualifying insured is the insured under the base policy.
443	
444	                                                                          Page 13 of 20
445	 � Cash Value Associated with Eligible Death Benefit: In general, larger cash values will result in smaller actuarial discounts
446	     as larger cash values are correlated with lower future premium requirements. In addition, the cash value associated with the
447	     eligible death benefit determines the minimum benefit amount payable.
448	
449	� Type of Life Insurance Accelerated: In most cases, the actuarial discount will be much larger on term coverage than on
450	     permanent coverage.
451	
452	What are the procedures for making a claim?
453	
454	Reporting: You must provide notice that a covered insured has suffered a qualifying medical condition. This must be done within
455	365 days of the qualifying illness for the Accelerated Benefit Rider for Critical Illness. American National will provide you with a
456	claim form and will request records of the insured's medical history, diagnoses, and treatments. In your claim form, you will tell us
457	what percentage of the policy you wish to accelerate. In the event of multiple partial accelerations, proof of qualification may be
458	requested for each claim. If you reside outside of the United States at the time of the claim, you will be required to see a U.S. doctor
459	in order to accelerate your benefits
460	Offer: If we determine that benefits are payable, we will provide you with an accelerated benefit election form which will include
461	an offer for an accelerated benefit payment based upon your request.
462	Election: In most states, you will have fifteen days following the receipt of the accelerated benefit election form to accept our offer of
463	an accelerated benefit payment. You will be under no obligation to accept this offer.
464	
465	                                                                Page 14 of 20
466	 Is my doctor required to certify that the condition is permanent?
467	
468	No. But your doctor is required to complete a certification in regard to your health so that American National may determine if
469	you have suffered a qualifying illness. Note: California Residents making a claim under the Chronic Illness Rider may request that
470	American National have a licensed healthcare provider that is not employed by American National certify the chronic illness in lieu
471	of providing a certification from a licensed healthcare professional of their choice.
472	
473	How do I know if I should submit a claim?
474	
475	Submitting a claim for an accelerated death benefit is a personal decision that will be based on an individual's health and financial
476	condition. If you think you have a qualifying illness ask your doctor how it impacts your life expectancy. If you make a claim you will
477	never be obligated to accelerate your policy.
478	
479	Is the benefit amount calculated differently if the qualifying illness is Chronic or Critical illness?
480	
481	The calculations are performed exactly the same. The focus is on future expected mortality regardless of the illness. The reduction in
482	future life expectancy determines the amount of the available benefit.
483	
484	What are the tax implications of the riders?
485	
486	The receipt of an accelerated benefit payment may be a taxable event. The tax law pertaining to accelerated benefits can be
487	complicated. You should consult your tax advisor regarding the tax status of any benefits paid under these riders. The qualifying
488	conditions for the terminal and chronic illness riders are designed to comply with the Internal Revenue Code requirements for
489	favorable tax treatment under �101(g). Generally, the accelerated death benefit paid for terminal illness is treated like a regular life
490	insurance death benefit.
491	The amount of an accelerated benefit payment due to chronic illness that may receive favorable tax treatment is limited by IRS
492	Section 7702B (Treatment of Qualified Long-Term Care Insurance). You should consult with your tax advisor to determine how these
493	limits apply to your situation. We cannot manage these benefit limits for you.
494	The Accelerated Benefit Rider for Critical illness is not designed to receive favorable tax treatment. However, accelerated benefit
495	payments under the Accelerated Benefit Rider for Critical Illness may receive favorable tax treatment under Internal Revenue Code
496	Section 105(b) and 105(c).
497	Consult your tax advisor to determine the applicability to your individual situation.
498	
499	                                                                  Page 15 of 20
500	 If my policy is a MEC and I accelerate my death benefit, what are the tax implications?
501	
502	Again, American National cannot guarantee tax treatment under any situation, including a MEC. Terminal illness is typically treated
503	as a death benefit in which the MEC rule should not apply. Chronic Illness typically is classified under the long term care rules which
504	would indicate it may not be treated as a MEC. Critical Illness is likely to be treated as a MEC.
505	We have not seen any IRS rulings on the treatment of a MEC for a Chronic or Critical Illness and would advise you to consult your
506	tax professionals regarding taxability prior to accepting an offer.
507	American National cannot be responsible for how the IRS treats any Accelerated Benefit Rider.
508	
509	If I am taking out a policy for the minimum face amount ($25,000) that only includes the terminal
510	illness rider, once the account value grows to over $50K, can the chronic and critical riders be
511	added at a later date?
512	
513	No � riders are subject to the minimum face amount requirements. In this case, the chronic and critical riders would not be added to
514	the policy.
515	
516	If a client completes a face amount increase request, are the additional ABRs added once the
517	increased amount is approved?
518	
519	American National can add the ABRs when the increase is performed. California Residents will need to complete a supplemental
520	application for the Critical and Chronic riders when applying for the increase.
521	
522	                                                                Page 16 of 20
523	 What can I use the proceeds on?
524	
525	American National and the rider contracts do not have any restrictions on how the payment is used. The check is made out to the
526	policy owner and can be used for any purpose you choose. It is your money to spend how you want.
527	
528	Is a certain condition covered?
529	
530	Please see the policy form for qualifying illnesses covered under this rider.
531	
532	What is the qualification if I suffer from loss of limbs?
533	
534	The qualifying condition must be the complete severance of two or more limbs through or above the elbow or knee joint due to
535	trauma or accident and results in a reduced life expectancy.
536	Loss of limbs as a result of a disease process is excluded from this definition.
537	
538	Will a benefit be available to me under the Accelerated Benefit Rider for Chronic Illness rider if I
539	am able to perform some of the activities of daily living?
540	
541	The payment is not determined on the inability to perform the activities of daily living. The activities of daily living are part of
542	the definition of chronic illness which is required to be satisfied before any benefit amount is available under the rider. Once the
543	qualifying illness requirement has been satisfied then the benefit amount will be determined based on future expected mortality.
544	
545	Who determines the value of my accelerated benefit payment?
546	
547	First, the American National doctor reviews the claim to determine if it satisfies the qualified illness requirement. Then, he will
548	determine the future expected mortality. Then, the actuarial department calculates the accelerated benefit payment.
549	
550	Can I apply for acceleration of a specific dollar amount or percentage? I need a specific dollar
551	amount, how do I apply for that?
552	
553	The accelerated benefit request should be for the death benefit the policyholder wishes to accelerated. After an accelerated benefit
554	quote is calculated the claims department can help roughly determine what percentage would need to be accelerated to provide a
555	certain dollar payment, if possible.
556	
557	                                                                  Page 17 of 20
558	 Why isn't the available accelerated benefit related to the amount of my medical expenses?
559	
560	The payment is in no way tied to the cost of medical expenses. The value of the accelerated benefit is based on the value of the
561	life insurance policy and the insured's future expected mortality.
562	
563	Are there any benefit limitations?
564	
565	Yes. Accelerated benefits will not be paid for conditions resulting from self-inflicted injury or attempted suicide. Accelerated benefits
566	will not be paid for qualifying illnesses that occur prior to the issue date.
567	
568	May I accelerate only a portion of the eligible death benefit?
569	
570	Yes. You may accelerate any percentage of the policy you choose, subject to the maximum total death benefit available for
571	acceleration. There is no minimum amount to accelerate and you may take multiple partial accelerations; however, the minimum
572	face amount on the issued policy must be maintained in order to keep the policy in force. Although proof of qualification may be
573	required at each time of claim, recertification is not necessary should you elect installment payments. The policies are also eligible
574	for full acceleration.
575	
576	What happens if the insured dies before the owner receives the accelerated benefit amount?
577	
578	If the insured dies after reporting the claim, but before receiving the accelerated benefit amount, then we will rescind the offer to pay
579	an accelerated death benefit and pay the death benefit pursuant to the terms of the policy.
580	
581	Will receipt of an Accelerated
582	Benefit lump sum distribution
583	impact my eligibility for government
584	benefits?
585	
586	An Accelerated Benefit payment may affect
587	your eligibility for government benefits. The rules
588	for eligibility for government benefits such as
589	Medicaid, supplemental security income, etc.
590	can be very stringent with very low maximums
591	as far as income and assets are concerned.
592	Many of these rules vary from state to state.
593	Consult an independent advisor to determine
594	the impact on your eligibility before applying for
595	Accelerated Benefits.
596	
597	                                                                Page 18 of 20
598	 Once I elect a payment option, can that option be changed prior to the release of the proceeds (e.g.
599	changing from installments vs. lump sum)?
600	
601	You can choose to change the option prior to payment. This change can be requested via fax or written email instructions. Once
602	proceeds are released as either installments or lump sum, the payment option cannot be changed.
603	
604	How long does it typically take for me to receive my benefit once all processing has been completed?
605	
606	Typically, claims will be processed within seven to ten business days.
607	
608	Is it possible once a claim is made for a chronic illness and later filed again for the same condition
609	under the critical illness rider if my condition worsens?
610	
611	Each claim is evaluated based on eligibility under the rider. You can submit multiple partial accelerations; however, each claim must
612	be eligible at the time of each claim submission.
613	
614	If I applied for a policy with an initial face amount of $500,000 but reduced the face amount to
615	$250,000 and then had a qualifying condition one month later, can I accelerate the higher face
616	amount since I paid premiums for the $500,000 face amount for most of the time I had the policy?
617	
618	You are only able to accelerate funds from your policy based on the face amount at the time of the requested acceleration,
619	regardless of the previous history of your policy.
620	
621	                                                                  Page 19 of 20
622	     Accelerated Benefit Rider Notice:
623	
624	      Policy Form Series: ABR14-TM; ABR14-CH; ABR14-CT. Forms will vary by state and may not be available in all
625	      states. Have your agent refer to rider forms for a complete list of illnesses and definitions. Some states may limit the
626	      definition of terminal illness to conditions that are expected to result in death within 12 months. The riders are subject
627	      to an administrative fee of up to $500 and an actuarial discount that will be assessed when the benefit is elected.
628	      Outstanding Policy Loans will reduce the amount of the benefit payment. The accelerated death benefit is an unrestricted
629	      cash payment. The Chronic and Terminal rider are intended to receive favorable tax treatment under 101(g) of the IRS.
630	      Receipt of a benefit could be a taxable event. You should consult a tax advisor regarding the tax status of any benefit
631	      paid under these riders. Receipt of Accelerated Benefits may affect your eligibility for Medicaid, supplemental security
632	      income, or other governmental benefits or entitlements. Before accelerating any benefit you should consult an advisor to
633	      determine the impact on your eligibility. Accelerated Benefit Riders are not replacements for Long Term Care Insurance.
634	      This is a life insurance benefit that also gives you the option to accelerate some or all of the death benefit in the
635	      event that you meet the criteria for a qualifying event described in the policy. This policy or certificate does not
636	      provide long-term care insurance subject to California long-term care insurance law. The policy or certificate is not
637	      a California Partnership for Long-Term Care program policy. This policy or certificate is not a Medicare supplement
638	      policy.
639	      Accelerated benefit riders ("ABR") and long-term care insurance ("LTCI") provide different types of benefits. An ABR allows
640	      the insured to access a portion of the life insurance policy's death benefit while living and may be used for any purpose.
641	      LTCI provides reimbursement for necessary care received due to a prolonged illness or disability. Coverage may include
642	      reimbursement for the cost of a nursing home, assisted living, home health care, homemaker services, adult day care,
643	      hospice services or respite care for the primary caretaker. The benefits may be conditioned on certain requirements or
644	      meeting an elimination period or limited by type of service, the number of days or a maximum dollar limit. Benefits under
645	      some ABRs and all LTCI are conditioned upon the insured not being able to perform two or more of the activities of
646	      daily living or being cognitively impaired. California: See form 10741-CM for a more detailed comparison of benefits
647	      provided by an ABR and LTCI.
648	      Neither American National Insurance Company nor its representatives give legal or tax advice. Agents can only discuss general
649	      benefits of conversion and must refer clients to their tax advisor or attorney for tax or legal advice.
650	
651	      American National Insurance Company, Galveston, Texas.
652	
653	  Not FDIC/NCUA insured Not a deposit Not insured by any federal government agency No bank/CU guarantee May lose value
654

content.thinking: I'm realizing this document is actually from American National Insurance Company in Galveston, Texas—not National Life Group or LSW at all. It's a competitor's ABR guide, so it's off-topic for the specific research question, though I should still extract any claims that might be relevant to understanding the broader context. This is a primary source from American National, not NLG, so I need to flag the misattribution clearly. The sourceQuality should be "primary" since it's an insurer's own guide, but the claims need to reflect that these ABR details belong to a different carrier and are tangential to the NLG/LSW research question. I'll extract the central finding that this is American National's form 10743 from January 2016, then note the ABR specifics as potentially confusable but not actually bearing on NLG facts.

content.text: This document is misattributed. It is published by American National Insurance Company (Galveston, Texas), form 10743, NOT National Life Group / LSW. This is a critical finding for the research synthesis — the ABR details here belong to a competitor and must not be attributed to NLG/LSW.

claims.claim: This document is a product of American National Insurance Company, NOT National Life Group / LSW — despite the source list titling it a 'NLG guide, IM10743'. It should not be used as an NLG/LSW primary source.
claims.quote: American National Insurance Company, Galveston, Texas.

claims.claim: American National's Accelerated Benefit Rider suite comprises three riders — Terminal Illness (Form Series ABR14-TM), Chronic Illness (ABR14-CH), and Critical Illness (ABR14-CT) — offered for no additional premium. (This is American National's structure, not NLG/LSW's, which uses form series like ICC and different rider forms.)
claims.quote: The Accelerated Benefit Riders are offered for no additional premium; however, the accelerated benefit is reduced by an amount calculated based on American National's evaluation of the insured's future expected mortality

claims.claim: American National's ABRs carry no separate premium but the payout is reduced by an actuarial discount plus an administrative fee of up to $500 (which may vary by state), and the actuarial discount is much larger on term coverage than on permanent coverage.
claims.quote: In most cases, the actuarial discount will be much larger on term coverage than on permanent coverage.

claims.claim: Under American National's design, terminal illness is triggered by a life expectancy of 24 months or less (some states limit to 12 months), and chronic illness requires inability to perform 2 of 6 ADLs for at least 90 days or severe cognitive impairment.
claims.quote: If an eligible insured has an illness or condition that is expected to result in death within 241 months or less.
